serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mango Salsa Soul Fire Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising a grilled spiced chicken portion (approximately 200 g) and a fresh mango salsa accompaniment (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Grilled chicken (skinless) and fresh mango — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) and Mangos, raw (NDB 09176), combined in a 2:1 ratio by weight to represent the plated dish.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Mango Salsa Soul Fire Chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: approximately 67% grilled skinless chicken breast (NDB 05013) and 33% raw mango (NDB 09176) by edible weight, reflecting the typical plated composition. Spice marinade and chilli contribute negligible macronutrient mass at typical usage levels. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed chicken and mango dish estimated from analogous components: grilled chicken (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and fresh mango (GI 51, Atkinson et al. 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values). Composite GI weighted by carbohydrate contribution of each component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mango Salsa Soul Fire Chicken. GI is estimated as approximately 50 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being fresh mango (GI 51 per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). Grilled chicken contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect the composite GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (50 × 29.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g serving provides an estimated 630 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake against the recommended limit of 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by mango-derived sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 14.7 per serving is in the moderate range; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish as part of a balanced meal, pair it with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor post-prandial blood glucose, as the natural fruit sugars in mango can raise blood glucose meaningfully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein source with low saturated fat and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled skinless chicken breast is a lean protein with low saturated fat (approximately 2.1 g per 300 g serving), and the mango component contributes vitamin C and potassium, which support vascular health; this dish is generally suitable for a heart-healthy diet when prepared without added butter or cream-based sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Salsa Soul Fire Chicken is a contemporary fusion dish served in Malaysian-influenced casual dining and café settings in Singapore, notably at EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street. The dish features a grilled or pan-seared chicken portion that has been marinated in a bold spice blend — typically incorporating chilli, garlic, cumin, and paprika — giving it a fiery, smoky character. The chicken is served alongside or topped with a fresh mango salsa made from diced ripe mango, red onion, coriander, lime juice, and fresh chilli, which provides a bright, sweet-acidic counterpoint to the heat of the chicken. The combination of textures — tender, juicy grilled chicken against the crisp, refreshing salsa — makes this a popular choice for diners seeking a flavourful yet relatively light protein-forward meal. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 18 g per 100 g), moderate in carbohydrate (approximately 10 g per 100 g, primarily from mango sugars), and low in saturated fat. The mango component contributes vitamin C, vitamin A, and dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at around 14.7, driven by the natural fruit sugar content of mango. Clinically, the dish is broadly suitable for most dietary patterns but warrants attention to sodium content from the spice marinade in hypertensive individuals, and to the mango-derived carbohydrate load in those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable and gluten-free in its standard preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Mango Salsa Soul Fire Chicken exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA SR Legacy composite of grilled skinless chicken breast and raw mango in a 2:1 ratio. GI is estimated from the analogous mango GI value (University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05013 grilled chicken breast and NDB 09176 raw mango) in a 2:1 weight ratio. Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish has not been performed. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the USDA SR Legacy source data for grilled skinless chicken breast and raw mango, neither of which contains industrially produced trans fats. 4. GI is estimated at 50 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being fresh mango (GI 51, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); this is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. 5. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g includes a contribution from spice marinade seasoning; actual sodium content will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The cuisine tag 'Malaysian' is preserved from the input record; the dish as served at EC Coffee Bar, Carpenter Street, Singapore, represents a Malaysian-influenced fusion preparation. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy composite and are approximate. 8. Halal status is assumed based on standard chicken preparation without pork or alcohol; actual halal certification status at EC Coffee Bar has not been verified.
